sentences.text: Yeah.
I'm coming to that.
Before starting Kolotex, I discussed with John Laos, Jan Laos, that we are not going to work for six months.
We are going to look around and see what we are going to do.
So we walked around for exactly two weeks.
And we had been told that there are some agents that could tell us what businesses are available.
By a [INAUDIBLE], John was an expert in yarns.
I think I was an expert in hosiery.
But starting from scratch, there's a lot of difficulty.
So we met this one agent-- and I don't know what the name was-- who was selling all sorts of businesses.
He had quite a number of sandwich shops.
He also had milk bars.
Milk bars, there was no such-- milk bars, coffee shops, et cetera, what he had.
We met somebody who said, why don't you go to a very well-known accountant, which was called Hungerford and Spooner?
They always have some business to go with.
So we went to Hungerford and Spooner.
And they told us the business which we have got at the moment is a-- a company, which is close to liquidation.
It was a quick frozen company called, Ready Made.
It is in Bathurst.
What are they doing?
They are making quick frozen peas, quick frozen carrots.
And that's what they are making.
And I said to John, let's have look at it.
So we went to Bathurst, which is only a drive of, at that time, of two hours.
John had the Morris Minor.
And so we went together.
Because later on, I got my Chevrolet.
I didn't come here as a schnorrer.
I was-- I wouldn't call it well-off, but I was OK.
So we went to Bathurst, and we had a look at the factory.
And there were working there about 120 people, to make carrots and peas.
So we went back.
And I said to John, Jan Laos, we have to send a-- a telegram to my sister to send us everything which she knows.
Because in America, it was much more advanced in quick frozen food than we were.
I want all the books I want.
So I sent a-- a telegram to my sister, Anna-- who was in the previous 30 minutes-- in Canada.
Went to New York, send me all the books you have got about quick frozen food.
We went-- we went back to Hungerford and Spooner.
We said, we'd like to do it, a week later.
But the company was a 250,000 pounds company, which, at the time, in 1954, was a huge company.
And they had 36,000 investors.
And we told them, after a week, we'd like to do it, but we don't want to take over the company.
We'd like to be their distributors.
If I say a week, it could have been 10 days.
So we were the distributors for Australia and for whatever. 10% of whatever turnover it was.
We started with-- of the 100-odd people which worked there, we sacked 80 of them.
And they had, in Matraville, a storeroom, which we took over.
And they had 20-odd people.
We didn't have-- we didn't need anybody.
So we started making peas, and we started making what we were supposed to take of whatever they made on a 10% commission.
So the-- the machines over there worked very hard.
We saw ourself-- we put in 30,000 or 40,000 pounds.
It was gone in no time, because the stock was there.
We couldn't sell it, because we made these little pieces, you know, these little-- whatever, called packets, which went to-- into the delicatessen shop.
And these delicatessen shop had these units which was from-- not from us, which was from the famous company.
What was the famous company here at that time, which put on these units?
You just-- can you give me a hint?
Can you give me a hint?
It was a famous company.
And they were obliged to take that in.
It wasn't our company.
We-- we called ourselves Ready Made.
Just give me a hint.
It was-- I-- I can't help.
What?
Bird's Eye?
Findus?
Birds Eye.
So Birds Eye put in these units.
Thank you, Nicole.
Birds Eye put in these units, and we couldn't put in our glacier foods.
So there we were.
And we had a stock of-- I don't know how much.
Our 40,000 pounds were down the drain.
And then, we-- somebody, one of us, John or myself, had a-- a brain wave.
We are going to supply the hotels or the restaurants, which bought packets, which was 12 pounds.
And this is where we went to.
So we covered the 40,000 pounds within a few months.
We supplied every hotel in the city.
We supplied all the restaurants in bulk packets.
On top of it, we engaged a Polish chef from the-- thank you, Nicole, for-- all right?
And we engaged a Polish chef from a well-known restaurant in-- in-- in-- in the-- in King's Cross.

And I also know the name-- I forgot the name of the restaurant, but he was-- and we asked him to come and join us in Bathurst one day a week, to make Weiner Schnitzel, to make goulash, so make it quick frozen food.
So after six months, we got into the state where we made profit.
We made profit.
And we were ready, because at 10%, we lost the 40,000 pounds.
We got back into profit.
And then, Hungerford and Spooner, which were the liquidators-- and the fellow was called Jackson.
I don't know his first name-- Fred, or whatever his name was.
And he said to us, now, you can buy the company.
I said, we are not ready for it.
We had already distributors in Victoria.
And we were-- on the way out, he said, you can buy for 250,000 pounds.
I said, no.
Then came along a company called, Edgell.
Being on the go, and we were really out of it.
We couldn't-- we didn't want to get that place because, honestly, I didn't understand it.
Jan Laos knew more about it than I do, because he's a handyman.
And he knows, food, and everything.
And we got out of that.
And then came the moment-- we thought the time has come where we get back into yarns and to hosiery.
So how to start a hosiery company?
What year was that?
What year was that?
That was still in the-- at the beginning of 1954.
Right?
And we go, and we registered the company, called Kolotex.
Kolotex stands for Kornmehl, Laos, Textile.
Right?
And we registered that name.
And we wanted to import machines.
And they told us, no import licenses.
So we thought we establish the name, we import from Holland.
And we establish the name, and we import from Holland, and we just get it around, which we did.
We were in The Strand Arcade.
Then later, on York Street.
We had a very nice office and a show room.
Very nice-- I mean, for that time.
And when I came, the nice people were David Jones.
[INAUDIBLE] were not so nice.
And then, we had an import from Holland from our factory, from my mother's factory in Holland, and called it Kolotex.
We couldn't import it.
We had to pay to-- we didn't have import licenses, so we had to pay, first 10%, 7.5%.
[INAUDIBLE] they were very nice to us.
And I'd like to have that on the video-- were Swift and Company.
They said, we are not going to charge you 10%, or 7%.
You pay 5%.
You pay it in 30 days, or 60 days-- Swift Company.
I think, that is, if you show it to some Australians-- because some Australians were very, very nice, Nicole, right?
And it was very, very nice.
Anyway, we imported.
And we had the resistance was rather not pleasant, because the buyers in Australia are not buyers.
They talk about their holidays.
They talk about their days off.
But David Jones, he wanted me to go out into the next-- we are ending to the next tape.
You know that.
It was very pleasant.
The one which we had the most pleasant one was John Martin Adelaide.
Do you want me to show them that?
I can show them.
Right?
Do you want me to do that, or not?
You just ask me questions.
Tell-- tell us the-- the major successes of your time with Kolotex?
My major success was that I met-- I was advised-- I was advised to go to meet the Liebermans in Melbourne.
The Liebermans had a factory, and I was advised to get into partnership with the Liebermans.
The Liebermans were very successful-- not really.
They had a factory, and we had the name.
Kolotex was already a name in Australia.
They didn't have a name.
They made stockings.
So we got together in 1959-- '58, '59.
We got together, and it was a very pleasant relationship, until-- now I have to be very careful-- 1962.
And we wanted to be public, so I went-- I bought, what we call, the back door entrance toward Roslyn Hosiery-- national hosiery mills.
We were the public company.
And a public company, you can't sell anything for cash.
And I want to leave it as such, because it doesn't make sense to go any further.
Because then, I went to the Liebermans, and I said, we can't be together.
I paid them out, in 1963, 380,000 pounds.
And the Liebermans bought, with 380,000 pounds, for 20 million pounds real estate, because it was the lowest ever in Australia-- '63, '64.
And then, it was a chance which I had, because both of them were in a gamble, whatever it was.
What could they lose?
Because at the time, it was possible to buy something for 10% deposit.
They had 15% deposit.
And I went into-- I got-- I-- I-- they-- we were leaving each other's company in 1963.
And I was together with Jan Laos.
And by that time, we were in-- you know, how did we get the machines?
I forget to tell you.
There was a Bonds Hosiery Mill in Rosebery, where we can only import machines for replacement, because we had these giants-- Prestige, Holeproof-- making stockings.
You had Beaumont.
You had Nightingale, et cetera.
They didn't exist anymore.
After Kolotex got big, we wiped them off them market.
But that is, we bought an old machine, old hosiery machine, once in Rosebery.
And we-- and we went for a round-- you know, a fully fashioned machine, we changed it into round machines, you know?
I don't know if you understand it.
But anyway, circle machines, because you're not wearing anything with a seam here.
They were fully fashioned machines, but with a seam.
So anyway-- so then, we bought this factory in Adelaide-- in Adelaide-- in-- what am I talking about?
In Sydney, in Leichhardt, which was the national hosiery mill, which was with fully fashioned-- it was not fully fashioned.
They also had fully fashioned machines.
We threw them out, and we had circle machines.
We were the first one in Australia and in the world to make pantyhose.
We first had-- if you remember that-- this was before your time-- which were called the 'stay-ups'-- which were some stay-ups.
We made the circular pantyhose, the circular stockings, then, later on, circular machines.
And it was an enormous success.
Enormous success.
We were very successful, after I got rid of the Liebermans.
The Liebermans-- I can't tell you.
They were very nice people, I'm sure.
I'm sure they are very nice, but I'm happy I was with Jan Laos.
Unfortunately, he got a heart problem.
Jan Laos got a heart problem in 1966 or '67 and had to leave the company.
We acquired Glomesh.
We acquired Rocktex, [INAUDIBLE].
We acquired-- we-- well, we didn't acquire that.
We made our own yarn mill in Seven Hills.
And we were the most successful-- we were the gold stock on the stock exchange.
That was Kolotex.
Then, one day, it happened.
It was 1973 that the people which got the first really money from us.
When we joined them, the Liebermans, they didn't have any money.
They were just bluffing.
And I don't mind putting it on video.
And I'm going to send it to them.
And they got from us 380,000 pounds.
They stabbed me in the back.
They came back to me and took over a company.
And they paid $1.87 [INAUDIBLE] public company-- $1.87 for a company which was worth $22 a share.
Right?
And this is my story about Kolotex.
I was very upset.
I was very sick-- sick for six months.
I couldn't think or dream anymore.

It was, fortunately, to a friend of mine-- his name was Kevin Welden from Paul Hamlyn-- who got me out a situation where I was, for six months, or nine months, in a state, having one or two bottle of scotch a day.
I didn't eat anymore.
And he hit me on my back-- the typical Australian way-- he said, get off your bloody so and so.
Right?
And that got me out of it.
Are we there, or not?
You're not there yet.
Right?
You want me to go on?
Anyway, so that was it.
Then afterwards, after getting out of Kolotex, I was looking around.
I mean, there was one year where I was sick.
Then, I got into a business, which was the book business-- no.
Firstly, I was with Paul Hamlyn for about one year.
I even made painkillers for one year.
I went to Canada, and I started a hosiery factory.
The hosiery factory was in Quebec.
We got all the assistance of the government.
All this isn't.
We got the factory built for us.
And they did everything for us.
And I imported to Australia 800,000 dozen.
Because at Kolotex, we supplied Franklins, we supplied Woolworths, you know, all the different brands.
We supplied David Jones, we supplied John Martin's, under their brands.
And I imported that from Canada.
The most beautiful pantyhose.
They were selling it [INAUDIBLE] for $0.99, $1.29, $1.39.
I imported it for Franklins to sell for $0.39 and $0.49 cents.
What happened?
The next day, it happened they approached me from Melbourne.
A company-- I mean, I don't know.
There was some or other semi-sub-organization, a government organization.
Said to me, how can you import and sell for that price?
I don't know.
And within three or four months, they told me, it's now on a quota system.
The quota goes to Kolotex, not to me.
I found out, later on, they-- they, you know, bribed Melbourne.
They bribed somebody in-- in-- in Canberra.
They bribed the whole, bloody lot.
And I was on a quota.
And the quota went straight to Kolotex-- back to Kolotex.
And they started importing too, not from Canada, but from Eastern Germany, where they got it for nothing.
And that was the end of my deal in Canada.
I sold the factory in 19-- sorry, if I got the date wrong-- in 1977, or '78, to Burlington Hosiery Mills in America.
I built that company in six months.
I had the best machines, the best yarn-- German yarn-- the best yarn, and everything.
I built it up from nothing.
But I'll tell you something.
Burlington did very well out of that factory.
What did I do then?
I was here.
1977-- Right.
Let me think about that.
That is very difficult.
Can we stop this for a minute, or not?
You can't.
Anyway, OK.
That might be easier for me, right?
Because you're pushing me-- anyway, that's all right.
My children--
My son got married to [?
Joey. ?] And I don't really know when it was.
It was in 1975, or '76.
Yes.
They've got four children.
My daughter, Yvonne-- you know, at one stage, my children were all over the world.
My son was in London.
And he started in New South Wales, and he started in America.
He's a-- he's a-- he's a-- he's a-- that's with the highest degree in marketing and commerce.
My daughter, Yvonne, was a bit lost all over the world, but she was in Amsterdam.
So my son was in Philadelphia.
And my Anita had her teachers degree-- I think it-- my teachers degree.
In Sydney, she was a teacher for the Yeshiva.
I'm coming to that in my next tape.
But a teacher with the Yeshiva.
She was also-- then, at the Yeshiva, Robert Feldman helped me to find her a job-- oh, no.
She didn't need a job in-- she wanted to have a Green Card in Los Angeles.
So at one stage, one daughter was in L.A, and my son was in Philadelphia and London.
And the other daughter was in Amsterdam.
So I was really not very, very happy about the situation.
And I think that was the time when I divorced my wife number one.
Right?
And it could have been before that time.
I divorced my wife number one in 1981.
Right?
And was-- was-- I-- I just spoke to her this morning, so there's a happy relationship.
Now, the other thing which you like-- what else you want to know?
Tell me about your interests and your hobbies, your activities within the Jewish community.
Now, until recently-- until recently, I used to play tennis.
I was a keen skier-- keen skier.
I was in Holland.
When I was in Holland, played soccer.
I never played cards.
I'm not a gambler.
I'm a gambler in my own business.
My interest is I like music.
And-- and that's as far as it goes.
And-- and can I mention something else.
And this goes for my children, I can't do that, you know, what I like.
I can't do that.
Right?
What about your activities in the Jewish community, presently?
Oh.
Jewish community-- I'm very much involved.
I was involved with building the-- involved-- how does it go?
One, I called a synagogue, Central Synagogue.
I joined them when I arrived here, in Grafton Street.
And it was Felix Friedman who got me involved in the [INAUDIBLE] court.
I was-- I organized-- organized for them the-- organized for them the-- the overdraft.
I was talking to the architect, and so on.
I didn't have a fallout, until years later, which was with a man called Gulowski.
I might as well put it there.
Gulowski came home [?
Adelaide, ?] who didn't know me.
The board came to see me at my home, I should come back.
Joseph [?
Bradenaugh ?] organized a cocktail party to come back, but my-- the President of the Temple Emanuel, [?
Motel ?] [NON-ENGLISH], wanted me to join the temple, and gave me seven seats.
My seven were only five, because this is the old-fashioned way of going about the Jewish thinking.
Right?
OK?
Jewish thinking is you are five, and you get the odd one to come and join you.
Now, I'll go a step further.
You want to know what I did for the Jewish-- OK.
I-- Rabbi Feldman was my friend.
And I think I paid either for one or two rooms.
You want me to go further?
OK.
When I joined the Temple Emanuel, I paid for-- I don't know how many rooms.
I also paid for the kindergarten.
I'm not a very wealthy man.
Really, I'm not as wealthy as most of your friends and so on-- the Scheinbergs and so on, you know?
Your father, right?
I'm just a simple man.
Emanuel School, I paid for, I don't know for how many rooms, but I donated the kindergarten.
You want me to go on?
You tell us what you'd like to speak about.
The-- I have to tell you something.
This came up in the 1960s.
That was the-- the New South Wale-- New South Wale-- the New South Wales School.
Is it finished?
No, I shouldn't do that.
You have the microphone.
It was a school.
It was-- I donated 10 rooms in the name of the Kornmehls-- five rooms for the Kornmehls, and five rooms for Steiner.
Julian Steiner was my finance director in Kolotex-- sorry about this.
And Julian Steiner-- and I liked him very much.
And you will see some placards there.
Right?
And-- right.
Will I go on?
You want me to go on?
What I'm doing?
[INAUDIBLE] I think I'm doing my best.
You ask me questions that's very difficult.
What lesson should we get from the Holocaust?
I think we should tell our children and our grandchildren that what happened should never happen any more.
Never.
Right?
That's all I can say.
How does it affect me?
It affects me very much.
I'm not sleeping.
I'm on the heavy stuff-- you can imagine-- called Rohypnol.
And every other-- it affects us.
I'm very, very lucky that I don't go for-- I don't get meshuga.
You know that word, meshuga?
And that's it.
Right?
It's meshuga.
Are we there?
And I don't want to go there, because I know friends in Holland.
And when they come home, they go to the garage.
And the wife organized it so-- so many plates towards the wall, to get-- to quiet them down.
I must admit that I've got these moments, because not only [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] going further away from Holland to Spain, but also, when I came back.
All this together makes me very nervous.
Right?
We're going to stop the tape there now.
Thank you, Paul.
OK.
Thank you.
